item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is your legacy now.

Your article haa gone through a real redemption ARC.

I'm not sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Meh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: CREEPYpasta

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn, I wandered back to the site on a whim and all of a sudden ex-503 go un-ARCed? That's a pleasant surprise!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The deactivation code is exactly the same as the activation code, except that it also requires [DATA EXPUNGED]. It is absolutely vital that [DATA EXPUNGED] be performed.

Not a fan of this line. Feels like a case where the author just didn't know what to put there.

This gets a +1 from me though. Simple, to the point, and something different.

item.title: Re: Strega Nona?
item.content:encoded: Huh, I'd never heard of Strega Nona but you are right, this is very similar in concept. Interesting!

item.title: Strega Nona?
item.content:encoded:
This is based off of Strega Nona, isnt it?

  1. activation codes. I forget the exact phrase, but there is a "spell" to activate it in the story as well
  2. "To deactivate it, users must recite the activation code, and also [D.E]. It is imperative that the user [D.E.]." this is a reference to the fact that you would need to blow three kisses in the story, which the guy who used it did not know.
  3. "You make it, you eat it". The punishment given to the guy who accidentally destroys a town using the pot.

on another note, why expunge the deactivation requisite? anyone should be able to deactivate it in an emergency!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one gives me nostalgia in more ways than one. Fun and clever.

Plus one for the pasta pot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A healthy balance of pasta-based carbs is a healthy diet for the wiki, because we need more creepypasta.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
/me snaps my fingers, deleting all of the ARCs except this one.

Perfectly balanced, as all things should be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Balance has finally been restored to the wiki.

item.title: Re: Mod Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
There isn't an exact threshold. Most Arcs are either in deletion range or near it, and won't be de-arced because of the concern that doing so could put them near it. Beyond that, most arcs that are in the positive and in relatively good standing upvote wise are poor quality skips that were used as plot devices during the (in)famous incident Clef-Kondraki tale. Even then, none are close to the rating that this one had.

If you ever think a certain arc deserves to be mainlisted, feel free to reach out to me and the decision will be considered.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is still so good

item.title: Re: Mod Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
I feel like 100+ would be a good threshold for "maybe this should be mainlisted" if there is to be one.

I imagine it's totally in the purvue of the staff, though.

item.title: Hell yeah!
item.content:encoded:
So happy this is back! I honestly never quite understood why it was up for deletion at one point. It's a bit goofy and it has some issues, but I still love it.

My favorite part is that I wrote scp-4378 back in the day with no idea this article existed and only read it because someone compared my draft to it.

Pasta is truly an inspiration to us all.

item.title: Image Attribution
item.content:encoded:
Image is from Here: https://www.maxpixel.net/Spaghetti-Blow-Pasta-Noodles-Cooking-Pot-Cook-586219

Public Domain.

item.title: Re: Mod Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Seeing as one of my favorite -ARCs has risen from the grave, what exactly is the threshold, the least amount of upvotes needed for one to come back to the mainlist?

item.title: Mod Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
So for those of you unfamiliar with the Pasta Pot, this is an old SCP that had been delisted and archived for falling below the deletion threshold, but had an additional article that depended on it. Since then, it has climbed back up on its own to above +100, and so this article is being returned to the mainlist as is.

Please note that this picture and the one on the action report are going to need to be replaced for licensing reasons, but this article was never known for the picture anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
By all means, I should downvote this. Bad tone, lolfoundation antics… shouldn't this be more scrutinized?

And it's when I finish this article that I realize no, I can't downvote this; in-fact, I can't help but to upvote this, because it's cohesive and, most importantly, pleasing to read. Every part works with one another to create a whole greater than the sum of its parts.

Let's start at the beginning.

Analysis
now they see my blood, on their sneakers

503-ARC begins with a trope I've seen very little outside of Series 1: the Object-Class/Picture 1-2-Punch. 503-ARC's image, like most images on the site, is the first thing one notices when clicking the page, and it's… standard. It's a bowl of pasta, being used… much like a bowl of pasta. Then you get to the object class, typically the first line of text you see in any skip (barring the image caption, which, in this case, is standard):

Object Class: Safe (upgrade to Keter under consideration)

And now it's got your attention.

Being so close together, the object class and image can be unconsciously conflated with one another. Already, the reader has some preconceived notion about what they're in for. This is normally a bad thing: as The Great Hippo said, your reader's imagination will win every time.

Here, however, there's a clash: how can this pasta be upgraded to Keter, if it was once Safe? It doesn't look like a Keter; the only Safe turned-Keter that comes to mind is 1048, a poorly constructed stuffed bear which, let's be honest, is already a pretty big horror trope. But pasta?

The containment procedures play off of this: it's useful, enough that they'll bring it out during mealtime. They're not even securing it as much as they say they could!

Speaking of the containment procedures, this does something that I wish more articles actually tried: it depicts the object at rest in the Foundation's care. You get a vivid mental image of how this object exists within the universe, what it shouldn't be doing, and the impact it has on the Foundation. Of course, this once again contributes to the 1-2-Punch's anticipation; what does it do?

Of course, there's another, more subtle element. These procedures help classify the mood and tone of the article's setting.

What follows is a genuinely amusing foray into a world of lolfoundation and hidden dangers. Ideas are touched on for only as long as they need to be touched on, all springing out of what should just be a thing what does a thing. At no point does it clutch the hot potato long enough to blister, but by g-d is it impressing its stupid 12-year-old friends. All of this is taken to a logical and shockingly vivid conclusion: death by pasta.

I disagree with the assessment that this should be mainlisted, because ARCing it has provided more freedom than it could ever hope in the last days of Series 4. To me, 503-ARC is as much a standalone as 173, and I wish the wiki played loose enough to recreate these.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Most likely it would be re-ARCed due to the enduring popularity of its test log (unless that was downvoted too!) and a story or two written including the article, which would be embarrassing. But embarrassment is the worst that would likely happen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hypothetically speaking

If this is posted on the mainsite and is downvoted to deletion range, would it be deleted like any other article or given special status due to being an -ARC?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I’d support this proposal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find myself agreeing.

There's no reason to have a nearly +100 -ARC, or indeed any -ARC that's this far into the high digits. Staff can still do some minor cleanup if desired (this is well under staff purview).

Suggest making it SCP-4503, because why not?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it's high time it makes a return to the mainlist.
Read this one before I even had an account, love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty sure this is the highest-rated -ARC article there is. It's high enough that it may deserve a spot on the mainlist again?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It seems a certain popular YouTuber read this article…

https://youtu.be/B8y3SSmz4sg

Someone send an MTF.

(This is an old favorite of mine.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also referenced by The High Court with the Magic Army.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because the meatball of the article was the description.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because everyone should read Tomie dePaola's Strega Nona. And when they do, they will know that obviously you just need to blow three kisses into the pot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The truth is that there is no deactivation code. The secret pasta was within you the whole time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why in the world would you expunge the vital step for deactivating the INFINITE PASTA MACHINE?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I remember this from before I made an account. It was pretty funny, but I can see why it was gotten rid of in the mass edit, but I think it'll work now. The fallout has calmed down and this ARC is in the positive ratings, maybe someone should re-write this in a different number. (Not me though, I haven't even figured out the sandbox yet)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I understand the similarity to Keter Cakes but this definitely has different flavor - accompanying story (prank or self-activation?) and a subtle sense of humor.
Also I like spaghetti a lot and it makes me hungry whenever I see the picture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Put it in SCP-1322 and be done with it! (?

Also, made a small correction in

Any abuse of SCP-503 or containment breaches will met with increased security measures and possibly even indefinite lockdown.

for

Any abuse of SCP-503 or containment breaches will be met with increased security measures and possibly even indefinite lockdown.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Currently, there is no practical way to respond to SCP-503 should containment be lost in a catastrophic fashion.

How about napalm? If that doesn't work, I suggest more napalm. Possibly chainsaw cannons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dunno. I really like this SCP and I think there's more that could be put into it…but maybe not if it's at a high rating. :/

EDIT: Also, I remember someone saying that we could rewrite these -ARCs and this SCP I can really relate to since it's food I have everyday.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why rewrite this particular one? It's +58.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Uggggh…I really really want to re-write this article badly.

I think i'll keep it as one of my to-do list SCPs. Irealy want to make a story of of this but…

Argh >_< Fact: I have been eating pasta since I was 1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This really should be on the mainlist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now that there's another tale about this article, should it be added to the ARC text at the top?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, my original intent in writing this was as a reference to Strega Nona, which I had admitted was the main inspiration for the article. It stayed on for a while until it got hit by the "can't base SCPs off other fictional items" rule.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wish we had the old comments for this so we could see why people thought this needed to be downvoted to the point that it was arc'd. So that we could learn from how wrong those people were.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I personally prefer to think of it as something dirtier, and those nuns should've been ashamed of themselves…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh man, I thought this old article of mine was lost to the ages. Kudos to whoever recovered it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also known as Scantron's pockets.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Guys, this is based off of the book "Strega Nona". The [DATA EXPUNGED] is the three kisses in the air. That stops the flow of the pasta.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Insert creepypasta joke

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because this has an AWESOME story about it!

Definitely. I see nothing wrong with an SCP that gets its good stuff from a story.

That being said, I think this one differs from the keter cakes on a different level. Looking at the history of the object, it feels more like a witch's pot that was converted to make pasta and might still be able to conjure up nightmares than it does some embodiment of ironic, inexorable doom.

In short, although what the two are make them similar, how they feel make them different enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because this has an AWESOME story about it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Jokes aside, this is basically the same concept as the Keter Cakes: unlimited food that could eventually cover the world and kill us all. The Keter Cakes are a more interesting variant on the concept, to boot, so what would this really add to the main list?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I see no reason not to just give this a new number.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm more interested in what the Data Expungio variety looks like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: BUT! This is creepy pasta!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ehhh… we DO have the Keter Cakes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, if this gets rated highly enough, might it be brought back?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yup, I got the text & picture off the Wayback Machine, and then I just had to replicate the format and add a link to the retrieval log. Easy as pie pasta.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn, now I'm hungry!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found it on the Wayback Machine archive a while back, and it looks like someone kindly put it back up here in proper format.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, Mann! Always liked this one. How was the text recovered?
